text.title: Reranking — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules covering when to add a reranker, top-N capacity, latency budget enforcement, eval before adopt, fallback on provider outage.

rule.statement: The reranker MUST run AFTER first-stage retrieval, not as a replacement. Retrieve top-N (N ∈ [20, 100]), rerank to top-K (K ∈ [3, 10]).
rule.rationale: Cross-encoders are expensive (O(N) full forward passes per query). Running them as first-stage on a large corpus is infeasible. The two-stage pattern is the canonical resolution.

rule.statement: top_n MUST be sized to fit within the reranker provider's max input cap (Cohere: 1000; FlashRank: configurable; Voyage: 100). Setting top_n > cap silently truncates and biases reordering.

rationale.source: Cohere Rerank API limits; provider docs

rule.rationale: Exceeding the cap returns partial results; queries that depend on a doc beyond the cap lose the doc entirely. Pin top_n explicitly per provider.

rule.statement: Pipeline MUST track end-to-end reranker latency budget. If observed p95 reranker latency > budget, the system MUST either (a) reduce top_n, (b) switch to a faster provider, or (c) bypass reranker with a degraded-quality warning.
rule.rationale: Silent latency creep degrades UX. Explicit budget makes the trade-off visible and triggers corrective action instead of slow drift.

rule.statement: Adopting a reranker MUST be preceded by an eval on ≥50 labelled `(query, candidate_ids, relevance)` rows showing ≥10% precision@K lift over the same retrieval without reranker.
rule.rationale: Reranker cost is real (managed API charges, local GPU memory). Without measured lift on the actual task, the spend has no defence.

rule.statement: If the chosen reranker provider becomes unhealthy (5xx, timeout), the pipeline MUST fall through to either (a) a local lightweight reranker, OR (b) return the first-stage top-K with a `degraded: true` flag. Hard fail is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Managed reranker providers have outages. Without fallback the RAG product fails for the duration. Degraded quality > downtime.
